name: basic red vinaigrette
instructions:
MACERATE: microplane or crush a clove of garlic into red wine vinegar. Let sit for ~15 minutes to soften the raw garlic edge and pre-load the vinegar with garlic flavor.

BUILD: whisk in dijon mustard. Then slowly drizzle in olive oil while whisking to emulsify. Ratio is roughly 3:1 oil to vinegar, adjust to taste.

FINISH: salt and freshly cracked black pepper to taste.
notes:
The bare-bones red-wine vinaigrette. This is the workhorse — reach for it when you don't want the added complexity of ketchup (ketchup-vinaigrette), tomato pulp (tomato-vinaigrette), or dairy (cream-vinaigrette). Same samin-scaffolding root as the ketchup and tomato variants (macerate aromatics in vinegar, then build), but stripped to the essential four ingredients.

MACERATE THE GARLIC — SAME REASON AS ELSEWHERE IN THE VINAIGRETTE FAMILY: raw microplaned garlic is aggressive; the 15-minute sit in the vinegar tames it and infuses the vinegar with garlic flavor. Don't skip.

DIJON DOES DOUBLE DUTY — EMULSIFIER + FLAVOR: dijon is what makes this an actual emulsified vinaigrette rather than an oil-and-vinegar drizzle. The mustard binds the oil and vinegar so the dressing coats greens evenly instead of separating on the plate. It also adds a sharp savory backbone that keeps the dressing from reading one-note-vinegary.

RATIO IS TO TASTE — CLASSIC 3:1 OIL TO VINEGAR IS THE STARTING POINT: some batches want more vinegar (going on assertive greens), some want more oil (going on delicate lettuces). Start at 3:1, taste, adjust. Dijon amount is also to taste — heavier gives you a sharper more mustard-forward dressing, lighter lets the vinegar and garlic lead.

SIBLING VINAIGRETTES:
- ketchup-vinaigrette: this dressing + ketchup + dill, pushed into american-cookout register.
- tomato-vinaigrette: red wine + balsamic + macerated shallots/garlic + grated tomato pulp — the italian samin version, no dijon.
- cream-vinaigrette: dairy path (heavy cream + white wine vinegar + raw garlic, no oil).
- strawberry-peach-vinaigrette: the sweet-fruit branch of the samin scaffolding.
- tangerine-mint-vinaigrette: juice-forward, splash-of-vinegar rather than vinegar-forward.
